coredata.dc:title: Conjugated polymer nanoparticles based on carbazole for detecting ferric ion (III) with a large Stokes shift and high sensitivity and the application in cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Zhi-yan
Zhang, Xian
Xing, Shu
Lu, Qian
Yao, Jin-shui
Liu, Qin-ze
Qiao, Cong-de
Xie, Rui-xing
Ding, Biyan
coredata.dc:description: The deficiency and excess of Fe3+ will lead to biological disorders and even many diseases. So, measuring the levels of Fe3+ in human body has drawn great attentions for health monitoring. Fluorescence method has been broadly applied because of high sensitivity, real-time detection and intracellular imaging. In this work, a new lipidic conjugated polymer, poly[3-{2-[2,5-Bis-(2-ethyl-hexyloxy)-4-propenyl-phenyl]-vinyl}-9-butyl-6-methyl-9H-carbazole] (PBMC), has been synthesized and used to prepare the conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs-PBMC) by coprecipitation with poly (styrene-co-maleic anhydride) (PSMA). The CPNs-PBMC exhibited large Stokes shift (141 nm), small average diameter (43.96 nm), low toxicity and excellent photostability. Meanwhile, the CPNs-PBMC was highly sensitive to Fe3+ and has a lower detection limit of 0.63 nmol/L. The sensing mechanism was based on aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of CPNs-PBMC in the presence of Fe3+ because of chelation and electrostatic interaction. Furthermore, the critical micelle concentration was measured by fluorescence method. More interestingly, the fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC could be circularly quenched and recovered using Fe3+ and EDTA, which may supply a possible control by logic gate. Furthermore, the fluorescence imaging showed that CPNs-PBMC could detect Fe3+ in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Conjugated polymer nanoparticles
Fe3+
Large Stokes shift
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of the fluorescence quantum yields
Cell culture and living cell imaging
Cell viability evaluated by the MTT
Preparation and characterization of PBMC and CPNs-PBMC
The fluorescence and absorption properties of CPNs-PBMC in different solvents
Fluorescence imaging in HeLa cells and MTT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of the CPNs-PBMC; (b) TEM image of the CPNs-PBMC; (c) AFM image of the CPNs-PBMC; (d) The particle size distribution of CPNs-PBMC obtained from DLS.

(a) Absorption spectra of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) to various metal ions (10 μmol/L) in water; (b) The absorption bar graph of CPNs-PBMC at 400 nm in presence of different metal ions, A and A0 are the absorption of CPNs-PBMC in the presence of different metal ions and absence of metal ions, respectively. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) in the presence of different metal ions (10 μmol/L); (d) Fluorescence intensity ratios (I0/I-1) of CPNs-PBMC at 557 nm, I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of CPNs-PBMC in the absence and presence of different metal ions, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) with the increased concentrations of Fe3+ in aqueous solution.; (b) Fluorescence intensity changes at 557 nm; Inset: the F0-F of CPNs-PBMC vs the concentrations of Fe3+. (c) Fluorescence quenching values (F0/F) vs the concentrations of Fe3+, Inset: Stern–Volmer plot of CPNs-PBMC upon addition of Fe3+.

The particle size distribution of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) obtained from DLS in the presence of Fe3+and EDTA; (a) Fe3+: 0.25 μmol/L; (b) Fe3+: 100 μmol/L; (c) Fe3+ (0.25 μmol/L) + EDTA (0.25 μmol/L). (d) TEM image of the CNPs-PBMC in the presence of Fe3+ (0.25 μmol/L); (e) Cycling tests of CPNs-PBMC by adding Fe3+ (0.25 μmol/L) and the same molar amount of EDTA.

Fluorescence images of CPNs-PBMC in HeLa cells in the absence or presence of Fe3+. (a1-a3) Confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with 10 μg/mL of CPNs-PBMC for 3 h. (b1-b3, c1-c3) Images of HeLa cells preincubated with Fe3+ (1 μmol/L and 10 μmol/L, respectively) for 1 h and then stained with 10 μg/mL CPNs-PBMC for 3 h at 37 °C.
The viability of HeLa cells incubated with different concentrations of CPNs-PBMC (0 μg/mL −50 μg/mL) for 24 h.

The mechanism diagram of Fe3+ detection by aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (AIFQ) based on CPNs-PBMC; The real photos of fluorescence recovery experiment were obtained under ultraviolet light, A: CPNs-PBMC; B: CPNs-PBMC + Fe3+; C: CPNs-PBMC + Fe3+ + EDTA (CPNs-PBMC: 10 μg/mL; Fe3+: 0.25 μmol/L; EDTA: 0.25 μmol/L).
The fluorescence and absorption properties of CPNs-PBMC in various solvents.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Λa max and λe max refer to the maximum absorption and emission wavelengths, respectively. CPNs-PBMC: 10 μg/mL; Fe3+: 10 μmol/L. Fluorescence quantum yields were determined using coumarin 307 (Φ = 0.56) as the standard.

head.ce:title: Conjugated polymer nanoparticles based on carbazole for detecting ferric ion (III) with a large Stokes shift and high sensitivity and the application in cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Amorphous and Polycrystalline Materials
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The deficiency and excess of Fe3+ will lead to biological disorders and even many diseases. So, measuring the levels of Fe3+ in human body has drawn great attentions for health monitoring. Fluorescence method has been broadly applied because of high sensitivity, real-time detection and intracellular imaging. In this work, a new lipidic conjugated polymer, poly[3-{2-[2,5-Bis-(2-ethyl-hexyloxy)-4-propenyl-phenyl]-vinyl}-9-butyl-6-methyl-9H-carbazole] (PBMC), has been synthesized and used to prepare the conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs-PBMC) by coprecipitation with poly (styrene-co-maleic anhydride) (PSMA). The CPNs-PBMC exhibited large Stokes shift (141 nm), small average diameter (43.96 nm), low toxicity and excellent photostability. Meanwhile, the CPNs-PBMC was highly sensitive to Fe3+ and has a lower detection limit of 0.63 nmol/L. The sensing mechanism was based on aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of CPNs-PBMC in the presence of Fe3+ because of chelation and electrostatic interaction. Furthermore, the critical micelle concentration was measured by fluorescence method. More interestingly, the fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC could be circularly quenched and recovered using Fe3+ and EDTA, which may supply a possible control by logic gate. Furthermore, the fluorescence imaging showed that CPNs-PBMC could detect Fe3+ in living cells.

A new lipidic conjugated polymer (PBMC) has been synthesized and used to prepare the conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs-PBMC) by coprecipitation with PSMA. The CPNs-PBMC exhibited large Stokes shift (141 nm), small average diameter (43.96 nm), low toxicity and excellent photostability, and could detect Fe3+ with high sensitivity (LOD = 0.63 nmol/L) based on aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (AIFQ). Moreover, the fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC could be controlled by logic gate using Fe3+ and EDTA. Finally, CPNs-PBMC was successfully applied to Fe3+ detection in living cells by confocal imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ion (Fe3+), an essential element for living organism, plays a vital role in many biological processes such as enzyme catalysis, cellular metabolism and oxygen transport [1,2]. Its deficiency can cause anemia and decrease immunity [3]. Meanwhile, excess Fe3+ will lead to lipids, nucleic acids, proteins and DNA damage [4–7]. According to “Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality of World Health Organization (WHO)”, the amount of Fe3+ is limited to 2 mg/mL [8]. At present, a variety of traditional analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [9], electrochemical analysis [10], colorimetric [11] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [12] have been adopted for the determination of Fe3+. However, these methods are generally time-consuming, complicated pretreatment procedures and skilled operation [13,14]. Fluorescence-based techniques have gained extensive attentions for the detection of Fe3+, due to the advantages including high sensitivity, fast analysis and online monitoring without any pretreatment of the samples [15,16]. Up to date, various materials such as fluorescence dyes [17], quantum dots [18], gold nanoclusters [19], have been utilized as fluorescence probes for the determination of Fe3+. However, they still suffered from some drawbacks in the fluorescence method mentioned above. For instance, some fluorescence probes are not conducive to the detection of Fe3+ in living cells because of the toxicity, low sensitivity or poor biocompatibility [20,21]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop some new fluorescence probes with low cytotoxicity, high sensitivity and excellent biocompatibility for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ in living cells.

Conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs), as a new class of fluorescence nanomaterials, have attracted tremendous attentions due to excellent brightness, photostability, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility [22–26] and achieved in a variety of applications, such as sensing [27], bioimaging [28] and drug delivery [29]. However, as far as we know, CPNs for detecting Fe3+ are scarce. Ding et al. demonstrated that solution-dispersible hyperbranched CPNs could selectively and sensitively recognize Fe3+ in tetrahydrofuran (THF) [30]. Yang et al. also found that the CPNs possessed aggregation induced emission (AIE) characteristics and further image captured for detecting Fe3+ in A549 cells [31]. Furthermore, fluorescence probes with large Stokes shifts (typically over 80 nm) play a key role in reducing error and maintain the accuracy of imaging [32,33]. Kumar et al. demonstrated that a photochromic spirooxazine derivative with a large stokes shift (110 nm) was used for detecting Fe3+ in methanol or methanol: water (1:1) solvent system [34].

At present, although CPNs have been widely studied, the poor solubility and biocompatibility of polymers were still a challenge. Carbazole and its derivatives is an important type of heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen-aromatic. They are used as pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, dyes, pigments, light-emitting photosensor, and so on [35]. Carbazole will be enlarged the conjugacy by Wittig-Horner and increase the fluorescence. A longer side chain was introduced into polymer to improve the solubility and enlarge the stoker shift [36]. It is benefited to improving the biocompatibility that conjugated polymers are modified with poly (styrene-co-maleic anhydride) (PSMA) to produce carboxyl group by hydrolysis [37]. Based on the above reasons, CPNs-PBMC were designed and prepared. As far as we know, CPNs with carboxyl groups as the active sites and large Stokes shifts for detecting Fe3+ have not been reported.

In this work, a lipid conjugated polymer, poly[3-{2-[2,5-Bis-(2-ethyl-hexyloxy)-4-propenyl-phenyl]-vinyl}-9-butyl-6-methyl-9H-carbazole] (PBMC) was synthesized, and its nanoparticles with carboxyl group were fabricated by a coprecipitation method with PSMA for Fe3+ detection. The optical properties of CPNs-PBMC were studied by a strategy based on fluorescence quenching due to the aggregation of CPNs-PBMC in the presence of Fe3+ (Scheme 1 ). Finally, it exhibited prominent properties of the large Stokes shift and high sensitivity. The cell staining ability of CNPs-PBMC was further demonstrated by detection of Fe3+ in HeLa cells.

All chemicals and solvents come from commercial suppliers without further purification. Biopharmaceuticals containing Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and poly (styrene-co-maleic anhydride) (PSMA) were purchased from Sigma. The number-average molecular weight (Mn) was ∼1700 by GPC, and the composition of styrene was 68 wt %. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments. All solutions of metal ions (Ag2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+) were prepared from their nitrates.

1H NMR spectra were obtained by a Bruker Avanace 400 spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was taken on a Nicolet Avatar 370 infrared spectrometer. Molecular weight and molecular weight distribution were got from gel permeation chromatography (GPC) of Heleos-II. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected on a UV-2500 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on F-7000 spectrophotometer. The particle size distribution of CPNs-PBMC and Zeta potential were determined on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) was given on a Multimode8. The morphology of the CPNs-PBMC was characterized on a JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The Surface Morphology of CPNs-PBMC was observed by scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) on Quanta 200. The pH value of the solution was measured with a PHSJ-4A pH meter. Cellular images were captured by a Leica SP8 confocal microscope.

The synthetic procedure of PBMC was shown in Scheme 2 . 9-Butyl-9H-carbazole-3,6-dicarbaldehyde (M1) and [4-(Diethyl-phosphinoylmethyl)-2,5-bis-(2-ethyl-hexyloxy)-benzyl]-phosphonic acid diethyl ester (M2) were synthesized according to the previous literatures [38,39], and their 1H NMR spectra were depicted in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, respectively. 1H NMR of M1 (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ (ppm): 10.10 (m, 2 H,-CHO), 8.77 (d, J = 9.8 Hz, 2 H, Ar-H), 8.06 (m, 2 H, Ar-H), 7.56 (d, J = 8.4 Hz, 2 H, Ar-H), 4.38 (m, 2 H,-CH2), 1.90 (m, 2 H,-CH2), 1.45 (m, 2 H,-CH2), 0.95 (m, 3 H,-CH3). 1H NMR of M2 (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ (ppm): 6.92 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 4.18–4.07 (m, 4H,-CH2), 4.04–3.92 (m, 4H, -CH2), 3.84–3.74 (m, 2H, -CH2), 3.21 (d, J = 20.2 Hz, 2H, -CH2), 2.02 (d, J = 5.1 Hz, 1H, -CH), 1.80–1.62 (m, 2H, -CH2), 1.47 (dddd, J = 27.0, 19.9, 13.5, 6.7 Hz, 4H, -CH2), 1.38–1.26 (m, 12H, -CH2), 1.22 (dd, J = 12.9, 5.9 Hz, 6H, -CH3), 0.96–0.80 (m, 6H,-CH3).

PBMC were generated from M1 and M2 by Wittig-Horner reaction. M2 (0.75 g, 1.11 mmol) was added in THF at 0 °C. Then potassium tert-butylate (also known as KTB) (0.20 g, 1.78 mmol) was added to THF and stirred for 30 min under nitrogen protection. M1 (0.30 g, 1.18 mmol) was added to the mixture and reacted for another 72 h at room temperature. Then, the mixture was poured into water and continually stirred for 20 min. The product was extracted with dichloromethane and purified by the fractional precipitation method with THF and n-hexane [36]. Finally, the yellow solids were obtained with a yield of 60.4%. 1H NMR in Fig. S3 (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ (ppm): 8.66–8.59 (m, 1H, Ar-H), 8.31 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 8.26 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 8.02 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 7.72 (d, J = 14.0 Hz, 2H, Ar-H), 7.51–7.43 (m, 2H, CH=CH), 7.42–7.33 (m, 2H, CH=CH), 7.01 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 6.86 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 4.36 (s, 2H, -CH2), 4.01 (d, J = 30.8 Hz, 4H, -CH2), 3.49 (d, J = 31.0 Hz, 2H, -CH), 1.89 (s, 4H, -CH2), 1.40 (d, J = 30.6 Hz, 16H, -CH2), 1.11–1.04 (m, 3H, -CH3), 0.98 (dd, J = 7.6, 3.5 Hz, 6H, -CH3), 0.91–0.86 (m, 6H, -CH3). FTIR in Fig. S4 (KBr) ν/cm−1: 1685 (C=C), 1598 (Ar-C), 1033 (C-O), 954 (CH=CH). Mn = 2.216 × 104 (±1.016%), Mω = 2.419 × 104 (±1.638%), Mn/Mω = 1.092 (±1.928%).

PBMC (0.5 mg) and PSMA (0.2 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of THF. The mixture was quickly added into 20 mL of water in a vigorous ultrasonic bath for 15 min. The solution was condensed to 10 mL by rotating evaporator and filtered with a diameter of 0.22 μm micro-filter to produce a yellow-green liquid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the fluorescence quantum yields
ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, the fluorescence quantum yields (
Φ
s
) of the CPNs-PBMC were calculated by the following equation (1):
(1)
Φ
s
=
Φ
r
(
A
r
/
A
s
)
(
n
s
2
/
n
r
2
)
(
F
s
/
F
r
)

Where the subscripts s and r represent the sample and the reference (coumarin307), respectively. The terms A and n are the absorbance of the molecules and refractive index of the solution. F refers to the integrated emission area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and living cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were cultured in confocal culture dishes with DMEM supplemented in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C. When the cell density reached 1 × 105 mL−1, the imaging experiment began to be implemented. The HeLa cells were incubated with CPNs-PBMC or CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ solution at 37 °C, and then washed with PBS buffer for three times. Fluorescence images were collected from 425 nm to 570 nm at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability evaluated by the MTT
ce:section.ce:para:
We used (4,5-dimethyl-2-thia-zolyl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay to evaluate the vitro cytotoxicity. After the cells were cultured in 96-well plates for 24 h, 10 μL of MTT were added to each well. After another incubation for 4 h at 37 °C, 100 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the purple crystals. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured by using a microplate reader. Relative cell viability was calculated by the following equation (2):
(2)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
(
%
)
=
(
O
D
s a m p l e
/ O
D
c o n t r o l
)
× 100 %
where OD
sample stands for the cells incubated with different concentrations of CPNs-PBMC, and OD
control refers for cells untreated with CPNs-PBMC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of PBMC and CPNs-PBMC
ce:section.ce:para: 1H NMR spectra in Figs. S1–S3 showed a new polymer of PBMC were obtained. Especially, the chemical shifts at 6.5–7.0 ppm indicated that the C=C double bond was formed by M1 and M2. In order to get a narrow molecular weight distribution, the stepwise precipitation method was adopted except delicate operations during the synthetic process. A satisfying result of polymer dispersity index (PDI = 1.016) was obtained. Considering the insolubility of PBMC in water. PSMA was introduced to improve the biocompatibility and supply active sites of carboxyl groups by the coprecipitation method with PBMC.

The CPNs-PBMC were characterized by SEM, TEM, AFM and DLS. As shown in Fig. 1 , CPNs-PBMC were mainly monodispersed, smooth and spherical morphology with an average size around 43.96 nm. Such CPNs could readily entry into cells by endocytosis [40]. Meanwhile, some small holes in CPNs-PBMC could be observed in Fig. 1(a), which implied that CPNs-PBMC may be used as a new platform for loading drugs [41]. The critical micelle concentration (CMC) was determined by fluorescence method. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity first enhanced and then reached a plateau with the increased concentrations of probe. The transfer point (CMC = 9.2 μg/mL) was got by the tangents of two trends. Moreover, the photostability of CPNs-PBMC in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were measured. The fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC and CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ was excited at 420 nm, and their fluorescence intensity at 557 nm were almost no change with the increase of time in Fig. S6. The results showed that CPNs-PBMC and CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ had good stability under 10 h of light. In addition, the Zeta potentials of nanoparticles were measured. In Table S1, one could find that the Zeta potential (−57.7 mV) of CPNs-PBMC in the presence of PSMA slightly reduced in comparison to that of pure CPNs (−52.1 mV) because of carboxyl groups, then increased after adding Fe3+(-45.3 mV), indicating that CPNs-PBMC could be adsorbed on the surface of Fe3+ through electrostatic interaction and chelation [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence and absorption properties of CPNs-PBMC in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and fluorescence spectra of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) in different solutions were measured at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm and shown in Fig. S7. The relative optical physical data were summed in Table 1 . From Fig. S7 and Table 1, one could find that the Stokes shift (141 nm) in aqueous solution was the largest in comparison with organic solvents, which allowed for the efficient separation of absorbance and fluorescence emission and was helpful to improve the accuracy of cell imaging experiments. The quantum yields were calculated by a reference method using coumarin 307 (Φ = 0.56) as the standard [43]. Among, the quantum yield of CPNs-PBMC in aqueous solution was about 15 times higher than that in the presence of Fe3+ (10 μmol/L), which indicated that fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC was quenched because of the interaction of CPNs-PBMC and Fe3+. CPNs-PBMC exhibited very distinctive fluorescence profile in acetone. Acetone is a good solvent for PBMC and PSMA, which will induce the polymer chains of PBMC and PSMA to stretch, and reduce a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process from carboxyl groups, and increase the fluorescence efficiency of CPNs-PBMC [44].

To demonstrate the selective applicability of CPNs-PBMC for metal ions detection, the absorption and fluorescence spectra of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) were investigated with the addition of different metal ions (10 μmol/L), including Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+. The results were shown in Fig. 2 .

As shown in Fig. 2(a), it was easy to find that the absorbance of CPNs-PBMC was greatly enhanced after adding Fe3+ in comparison to other metal ions (at least 8 times). Moreover, Fig. 2(b) could describe more clearly the selectivity of CPNs-PBMC for Fe3+ by the absorption bars at 400 nm. At the same time, the fluorescence data also showed the same selectivity. As shown in Fig. 2(c) and (d), the addition of Fe3+ could cause greatly fluorescence quenching of CPNs-PBMC. The absorbance and fluorescence intensity of CPNs-PBMC was similar in the presence of Fe3+ and the mixed ions, which indicated that CPNs-PBMC possessed a better anti-interference to detect Fe3+ in the presence of other metal ions. These results showed CPNs-PBMC was highly selective for Fe3+ detection.

The fluorescence titration experiments were carried out to explore the sensitivity of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) to Fe3+ [(Fig. 3 (a)]. The mixture of CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ was placed statically for 5 min before the spectral measurement.

The fluorescence intensity of CPNs-PBMC was gradually quenched with increasing Fe3+ concentrations and decreased 95.6% after adding 5 μmol/L of Fe3+ [Fig. 3(b)]. Among, there was a fine linear relationship of fluorescence intensity versus Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0.1 μmol/L to 1 μmol/L [Fig. 3(b) inset]. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.63 nmol/L, which was obtained by the equation LOD = 3Sb/K [45] (Sb represents the standard deviation and K is the slope between fluorescence intensity versus Fe3+ concentrations).

Further, the quenching constant (K
SV ) of CPNs-PBMC to Fe3+ was calculated by the Stern–Volmer equation (3):
(3)
F
0
/ F =
K
s v
× Q + 1
where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively [46]. Q is the concentration of Fe3+. The curve of F
0 /F vs the concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 0.1 mol/L to 0.45 mol/L was linearly fitted [Fig. 3(c) inset], while the plot did not fit a linear equation in the range of 0.1 μmol/L to 1 μmol/L [Fig. 3(c)], indicating that both dynamic and static quenching processes occur [31]. The K
SV was gained to be 1.18 × 106 M−1. This parameter was higher than those in previous reports (typical K
SV of about 104 M−1) [47]. This illustrated that CPNs-PBMC had a relatively high quenching efficiency to Fe3+.

The fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) and to CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ (1 μmol/L) was investigated under different pH conditions. The pH values were adjusted by NaOH and HCl, and the pH detectors was used to measure the values. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC was enhanced gradually with increasing of pH values in the range of 4.0–7.4 and then decreased in alkaline solution. The carboxyl on the surface of CPNs-PBMC should be responsible for these changes. However, the fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ was almost no change with the increased pH. So, the measures in this manuscript were finished at about pH = 7.4. Thus, it was also suitable for in living cells.

To explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism between CPNs-PBMC with Fe3+, the particle sizes of CPNs-PBMC and CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ were characterized by DLS and TEM. The average diameters of CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) were 62.19 nm and 1673.89 nm after the addition of Fe3+ (0.25 μmol/L) and Fe3+ (100 μmol/L) in Fig. 5 (a) and Fig. 5(b), respectively, which were much larger than that of CPNs-PBMC (43.96 nm) described in Fig. 1(d). Meanwhile, the TEM image of CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ in Fig. 5(d) also showed the same conclusion in comparison to the particle size of CPNs-PBMC showed in Fig. 1(b). The interaction mechanism was attributed to the aggregation behavior of CPNs-PBMC in the presence of Fe3+ because of chelation and electrostatic interaction, which caused quenching of fluorescence [48]. As described in Table S1, the Zeta potential change of CPNs-PBMC in the absence and presence Fe3+ indicated that electrostatic interaction and chelation played an important role. Furthermore, when EDTA (0.25 μmol/L) was added, the fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ (10 μg/mL + 0.25 μmol/L) was recovered obviously (the real photos in Scheme 1). The average particle size of system also decreased to 47.92 nm [Fig. 5(c)], which was close to the average diameter of CPNs-PBMC (43.96 nm). Specifically, cycling tests (five times) were operated by continuously adding Fe3+ and the same molar amount of EDTA to the solution of CPNs-PBMC. The results showed that the fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC could be circularly recovered in Fig. 5(e) and controlled by logic gate using Fe3+ and EDTA [30]. The interaction mechanism was attributed to the aggregation behavior of CPNs-PBMC in the presence of Fe3+ because of chelation and electrostatic interaction, which caused quenching of fluorescence. The addition of EDTA would capture Fe3+ from CPNs-PBMC-Fe3+ and promote the re-dispersion of nanoparticles to recover fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging in HeLa cells and MTT
ce:section.ce:para: The potential utilities of CPNs-PBMC were proceeded to examine by the fluorescence imaging of exogenous Fe3+ in living HeLa cells. The control cells were cultivated according to the above description and incubated with CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) for 3 h at 37 °C. Under the same condition, the other cells were preincubated with Fe3+ (1 μmol/L and 10 μmol/L, respectively) for 1 h and stained with CPNs-PBMC (10 μg/mL) for 3 h. The incubated systems were washed with PBS to remove extracellular CPNs-PBMC and Fe3+ before fluorescence imaging. The results were showed in Fig. 6 . The HeLa cells incubated with only CPNs-PBMC showed excellent brightness [Fig. 5(a1-a3)]. However, the fluorescence of CPNs-PBMC was obviously quenched after preincubation with Fe3+. Moreover, the degree of fluorescence quenching was obviously increased with the higher concentration of Fe3+ [Fig. 6(b1-c3)]. The results in cells were consistent to the conclusions in extracellular and indicated that CPNs-PBMC can be used as an effective Fe3+ imaging agent. At the same time, the cytotoxicity of CPNs-PBMC (0 μg/mL - 50 μg/mL) to the HeLa cells was evaluated by MTT assays. As shown in Fig. 7 , it showed that the CPNs-PBMC has no obvious toxicity for 24 h (survival rate ˃ 90%).

In summary, we have synthesized a new conjugated polymer PBMC by Wittig-Horner reaction and prepared CPNs-PBMC with carboxyl group. The experiment results showed that CPNs-PBMC was highly selective and sensitive towards Fe3+ with a lower LOD of 0.63 nmol/L and larger Stokes shift (141 nm) with excellent properties including good biocompatibility, low toxicity and better photostability. The sensing mechanism was verified by DLS, TEM and Zeta potential, which was mainly on the basis of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe3+ because of chelation and electrostatic interaction. Meanwhile, the periodic fluorescence quenching and recovering by Fe3+ and EDTA may supply a possible control by logic gate. Furthermore, the fluorescence imaging showed that CPNs-PBMC was suitable for detecting exogenous Fe3+ in living HeLa cells. The more biological applications of CPNs-PBMC would be explored because of its excellent properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51403111, 11774188) and Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR201808150173, ZR2017BH053). We are particularly grateful to the funding supporting from Qilu University of Technology for talents.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.04.030.
